sourceLocator: WHO 2022 global genomic surveillance strategy, goal and objectives section, pp. 4-9
excerpt: quality, timely and appropriate public health actions
passageSummary: WHO frames genomic surveillance as part of broader surveillance and laboratory systems, with a goal of quality, timely and appropriate public-health action. Its objectives cover geographic access, workforce, data sharing, utility for decision-making, connectivity across surveillance architecture, and emergency readiness.
limitations: High-level strategy source; not an operational implementation runbook.

sourceLocator: knowledge-base/source-cards/phe-implementing-pathogen-genomics-case-study.md; extracted text section 2, PHE Colindale WGS service
excerpt: extracted bacterial DNA is received, sequencing is performed and short read sequences delivered
passageSummary: The PHE case study describes a centralised high-throughput WGS service where extracted bacterial DNA is received, sequencing is performed, and sequence data are delivered to the requesting reference laboratory for further analysis.
limitations: Specific to PHE's centralized bacterial WGS service model.

sourceLocator: PHE case study, sections 3.1 Business and capital planning, 3.3 Workflow development, and 3.5 Staff training and engagement
excerpt: A sequencing service requires extensive IT infrastructure
passageSummary: The PHE case study describes business/capital planning, IT infrastructure, storage and processing requirements, workflow development from sample receipt to reporting, LIMS interaction with bioinformatics infrastructure, and staff training needs.
limitations: UK/PHE-specific implementation context; should be adapted carefully.

sourceLocator: PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript, Five factors for bioinformatics infrastructure
excerpt: which parts of the pathogen bioinformatics analysis solution do you want to be responsible for?
passageSummary: The PHA4GE manuscript introduces five infrastructure questions covering responsibility, workflow management, compute location, data flow, and access.
limitations: Infrastructure-heavy source; limited coverage of broader governance, repository policy, and economics.

sourceLocator: PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript, conclusion
excerpt: not achieved by CPU cores and terabytes of storage alone
passageSummary: The PHA4GE manuscript states that compute infrastructure must include workflow execution engines, data management systems, system configuration, and people to maintain the infrastructure.
limitations: Supports a general operating-model point, not a complete responsibility matrix.

sourceLocator: knowledge-base/source-cards/who-national-genomic-surveillance-strategy-2023.md; extracted text value-chain figure around lines 300-326
passageSummary: WHO's value-chain figure links priority genomic surveillance use cases to sample and data collection, genetic material extraction, sequencing, bioinformatics analyses, public-health analyses, interpretation with multisource data, data publication, result sharing, and public-health use.
limitations: Strategic value-chain figure; does not specify detailed operational thresholds or repository fields.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool, Figure 1 and section 2.3
excerpt: objectives, users, sampling strategy, outcomes
passageSummary: WHO places priority genomic surveillance use cases at the start of the value chain and says each identified disease or public-health priority should articulate objectives, data users, analysis and action routes, and expected outcomes.
limitations: Strategy-level framing; does not prescribe local operational templates.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool, section 2.2 principles
excerpt: Data quality and internal consistency must be given priority
passageSummary: WHO identifies multisource data integration, representative sampling, quick turnaround time, data quality, metadata completeness, and public-health reporting as principles for genomic surveillance strategy and data management.
limitations: Does not provide organism-specific sampling fractions, turnaround targets, or QC thresholds.

sourceLocator: knowledge-base/source-cards/who-national-genomic-surveillance-strategy-2023.md; extracted text data management section around lines 515-566
passageSummary: WHO describes data management as a key element for genomic surveillance and includes storage, backup, retention, data quality, metadata completeness, analysis and interpretation, privacy/security, and reporting for public-health action.
limitations: Supports planning categories, not a full local data architecture.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool, section 2.4 data management
excerpt: storage requirements should be sized according to the retention strategy chosen
passageSummary: WHO states that genomic surveillance data-storage policy should support short- and long-term analysis and archiving, be defined from available hardware, software, infrastructure and connectivity, include one or two backup locations depending on criticality, and size storage according to retention strategy for raw, filtered and processed data. The same section says data security must apply to local and cloud storage.
limitations: Strategy-level guidance; does not define local retention periods or storage sizing formulae.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool, section 2.4 data management
excerpt: should consider all data relevant to surveillance systems
passageSummary: WHO states that genomic surveillance data-management policy should not focus only on genetic sequence data, but should consider all data relevant to surveillance systems to support interoperability. The same section links data quality, internal consistency, metadata completeness, metadata quality, storage, privacy/security, and reporting for public-health action.
limitations: Strategy-level guidance; it does not define a complete local identifier schema.

sourceLocator: Armstrong et al. 2019, sections on bacterial foodborne illness, data integration, and NGS workflows
excerpt: Complex workflows are required
passageSummary: Armstrong et al. describe WGS as higher-resolution than PFGE, useful for understanding transmission and links between cases, but also requiring integration of laboratory, epidemiological, workflow, storage, and secure-sharing functions.
limitations: Review article, not an implementation runbook; examples are weighted toward established public-health programmes.

sourceLocator: Armstrong et al. 2019, workforce transformation section
excerpt: Public health workforce development is central
passageSummary: Armstrong et al. state that public-health genomics requires workforce development. They describe microbiologists needing microbial genomics knowledge, epidemiologists needing skills and tools to translate genomic data into public-health action, and both groups needing basic bioinformatics vocabulary.
limitations: Review-level source; does not define a staffing model or training curriculum.

sourceLocator: Armstrong et al. 2019, section on integrating laboratory and epidemiological data
excerpt: these data need to be integrated
passageSummary: Armstrong et al. state that laboratory and epidemiological data are often stored and managed separately, and that pathogen genomic data need to be integrated with epidemiological data to realise the value of both.
limitations: Supports the integration principle; does not prescribe a field-level metadata model.

sourceLocator: PHE case study, section 3.3 Workflow development and bioinformatics
excerpt: from sample receipt to the reporting of results
passageSummary: The PHE case study describes operationalising WGS at scale by considering infrastructure and workflow stages together, including sample management, LIMS interaction, bioinformatics pipelines, quality reporting, storage configuration, user access, and results returned to users.
limitations: Specific to a centralised PHE service model, but useful as a concrete service-design example.

sourceLocator: PHE case study, section 3.2 infrastructure and section 3.3 bioinformatics
excerpt: A sequencing service requires extensive IT infrastructure
passageSummary: The PHE case study states that a sequencing service requires IT infrastructure to manage and store sequence data and run analysis software, including expertise for a high-performance computing cluster, maintenance and migration of infrastructure, enough storage and processing speed to avoid service impact, high-performance storage, archive storage, and data-lifecycle management software.
limitations: Centralised UK service implementation; its specific infrastructure choices should not be generalised as universal requirements.

sourceLocator: PHE case study, section 3.3 Workflow development, specimen receipt and management
excerpt: The interaction between LIMS and the bioinformatics infrastructure
passageSummary: The PHE case study describes workflow development from sample receipt to reporting and identifies sample tracking, service-user tracking, LIMS interaction with bioinformatics infrastructure, data quality reporting, storage configuration, data access, sample-context integration, and report design as part of the end-to-end service.
limitations: Centralised UK service example; implementation details may differ in other systems.

sourceLocator: PHE case study, section 4.2 Genomics in action
excerpt: Resolution of outbreaks that were not being solved
passageSummary: The PHE case study reports public-health value from WGS through resolving outbreaks not solved by conventional methods, faster outbreak resolution, detection of cryptic outbreaks, stronger links between cases and sources, and improved understanding of transmission pathways.
limitations: Enteric bacterial reference-laboratory example from the UK; not a universal estimate of benefit.

sourceLocator: PHE case study, section 3.5 Staff training and engagement
excerpt: Specialised bioinformatics staff were needed
passageSummary: The PHE case study reports that switching to WGS required multidisciplinary working and training for laboratory staff, specialist bioinformatics staff, computing and software-development expertise, skill transfer to epidemiologists and clinical scientists, Galaxy access for laboratory staff, online learning resources, user letters and surveys, regional roadshows, and collaborations with other agencies.
limitations: Centralised UK service example; staffing pattern should be adapted to local service design.

sourceLocator: Price et al. 2023, abstract and discussion of systematic review of WGS economic evaluations
excerpt: limited by marked heterogeneity
passageSummary: Price et al. identified 681 articles, assessed 49 full texts, and included 9 studies. All included studies supported WGS surveillance on economic grounds, but evidence was heterogeneous, concentrated in hospital infection prevention/control and foodborne surveillance, and limited for low- and middle-income country implementation and real-world effectiveness.
limitations: Does not support a universal return-on-investment claim.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025 pathogen genomic data-sharing platform guidance, sections 2.4 data scope, 2.7 data provenance, and 2.9 interoperability
excerpt: auditable trail all along the sequence and metadata generation continuum
passageSummary: WHO's platform guidance defines minimum metadata as including sample information, high-level sampling strategy, sequencing strategy, bioinformatics methods, and attribution data. It also describes provenance as an auditable trail across sample collection, metadata collection, laboratories, sequencing, bioinformatics, submission, publications, entry route, and identifiers linking records across resources. Its interoperability section supports documented APIs and standards for exchange with other systems.
limitations: Written for data-sharing platforms; local service databases may need a smaller but compatible internal model.

sourceLocator: APHL 2016 Next Generation Sequencing Implementation Guide, Chapter 4 Validation
excerpt: Consider all steps in the validation process
passageSummary: APHL describes validation as part of NGS quality assurance and control. Its validation considerations include nucleic acid extraction, enrichment, library preparation, data analysis, alignment thresholds, accuracy metrics such as depth of coverage, number of reads and Q score, and robustness and reproducibility across operators and reagent lots.
limitations: Public-health laboratory implementation guide from 2016; examples are weighted toward PulseNet-style bacterial WGS implementation.

sourceLocator: Gador-Whyte et al. 2026, Clinical Microbiology Reviews, Quality management section
excerpt: applies to every step of the sequencing and analysis workflow
passageSummary: The clinical microbiology implementation review states that QC applies across the sequencing and analysis workflow, validation and verification are essential for new or introduced methods, genomic validation requires wet-lab and bioinformatics process validation, software and database version control, and PT/EQA should cover wet-lab and dry-lab components.
limitations: Clinical microbiology review; public-health surveillance services should adapt its diagnostic-laboratory framing carefully.

sourceLocator: Gador-Whyte et al. 2026, Clinical Microbiology Reviews, bioinformatics implementation and regulatory challenges sections
excerpt: each step requires QC
passageSummary: The review identifies barriers around limited bioinformatics expertise, lack of workflow standardisation, inter-laboratory variation in SNP and resistance-gene prediction, data storage and transfer, LIS limitations, unfamiliar bioinformatics QC and validation, local acceptance thresholds, limited EQA availability, and the need for shared standards, SOPs, and validation datasets.
limitations: Best used to identify service risks and quality-system needs, not as a complete public-health runbook.

sourceLocator: Gador-Whyte et al. 2026, Clinical Microbiology Reviews, key staff competencies section
excerpt: competencies relating to the genomic workflow
passageSummary: The clinical microbiology implementation review states that genomic wet-lab scientists and bioinformaticians need competencies across genomic workflow, QC and communication. It lists wet-lab, library-preparation, data-QC, data-analysis, interpretation, validation, accreditation, reporting and communication competencies across the workflow.
limitations: Clinical microbiology focus; public-health surveillance roles may need additional epidemiology, policy and programme-management competencies.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025 pathogen genomic data-sharing platform guidance, sections 2.6 data curation and 2.11 analytical/reporting capabilities
excerpt: annotated with quality control methods and results
passageSummary: WHO's platform guidance describes data curation as integrity, quality, and completeness checks. It states that low-quality or incomplete data may confound interpretation but may still be useful for validation or emergency sharing if annotated with QC methods and results. The analytical/reporting section states that integrated analytical tools should be documented and version-controlled.
limitations: Platform guidance; local services need to define which annotations affect public-health reporting, repository submission, and reuse.

sourceLocator: PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript, sections on data flow, IAM, and scope of responsibility
excerpt: ingestion, storage processing and delivery
passageSummary: The PHA4GE manuscript describes data flow through ingestion, storage, processing and delivery, while satisfying confidentiality, integrity and accessibility. It links data management to storage, access control, versioning, archiving, sharing, provenance, data-management plans, retention policy and role-based access. Its responsibility model separates SaaS, PaaS and IaaS by how much responsibility is handled by users, administrators or external parties.
limitations: Infrastructure-focused source; not a legal retention policy or detailed disaster-recovery standard.

sourceLocator: WHO 2024 genomics costing tool user manual, Bioinformatics worksheet section
excerpt: cloud computing costs, in-house server costs or a hybrid
passageSummary: WHO's costing manual states that bioinformatics costing can accommodate cloud, in-house server or hybrid models, including software licensing, storage, cloud costs, in-house hardware, maintenance fees, redundancy if selected for storage, and human resources such as system administrators and systems engineers.
limitations: The manual is SARS-CoV-2 focused in its current version, but the cost categories are useful for recurrent planning.

sourceLocator: WHO 2024 genomics costing tool user manual, Personnel and Training and Quality Management worksheet sections
excerpt: personnel involved in each workflow step
passageSummary: WHO's costing manual includes personnel and training costs across sequencing workflow steps, administrative and management personnel, quality and biosafety managers, laboratory directors, human resources, and adjustable percentage time. It also includes quality-management costs such as accreditation, proficiency testing, certification and other quality activities.
limitations: Current manual is SARS-CoV-2 focused and designed for costing, not workforce planning by itself.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 global genomic surveillance progress report, strategy results hierarchy and opportunities sections
excerpt: Strengthen the workforce to deliver at speed, scale and quality
passageSummary: WHO's progress report identifies workforce strengthening as a global genomic surveillance objective, including genomics and bioinformatics training packages, communities of practice, external quality assurance, knowledge exchange, costing and forecasting tools, data analysis and management resources, supply chains, and integration with surveillance and laboratory information systems.
limitations: Progress-report source; supports capability categories rather than a detailed staffing template.

sourceLocator: PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript, implementation comparison and discussion sections
excerpt: limited scalability, flexibility and structures
passageSummary: The PHA4GE manuscript compares several implementation models. It reports that the laptop example was useful where internet and power were unreliable but shifted management to the operator and had limited scalability and access-control structure. On-premises models met local needs but required expertise for job-processing systems. Cloud models reduced hardware barriers but required sufficient internet for data upload. The discussion cautions that infrastructure choices have long-lasting effects.
limitations: Useful for dependency and maturity discussion, but not a validated scoring rubric.

sourceLocator: WHO 2022 guiding principles for pathogen genome data sharing, scope and principles 1 and 8
excerpt: ethical, equitable, efficient, and effective
passageSummary: WHO's guiding principles state that pathogen genome data sharing protects global public health, supports epidemic and pandemic prevention, detection and response, endemic disease monitoring, and AMR tracking. The principles apply to rapid public sharing of pathogen genome data with appropriate non-sensitive metadata and support timely sharing, capacity development, equitable access to benefits, and an 'as open as possible and as closed as necessary' access approach.
limitations: Principles source; local implementation still requires jurisdiction-specific policy and platform decisions.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool, section 2.5 Promote data sharing and collaboration
excerpt: Data sharing is essential to inform risk assessment
passageSummary: WHO's national strategy support tool states that data sharing supports risk assessment and public-health decisions at local, national, regional and global levels, should be governed by a national pathogen genome data-sharing policy, and may include a national database that centralizes sequencing data and metadata while integrating with public genomic sequence databases.
limitations: National strategy source; does not prescribe a local platform architecture or legal template.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025 pathogen genomic data-sharing platform guidance, sections 2.1 governance, 2.8 access, and 2.10 data use and benefits sharing
excerpt: code of practice or terms of use, access policies
passageSummary: WHO's platform guidance states that governance should cover legal, ethical, privacy, access, licensing and accountability arrangements. Its access section distinguishes anonymous, account-linked, verified/vetted, restricted and closed access, with documented policies. Its data-use section covers licences, user responsibilities, acknowledgement, onward sharing, withdrawal or deletion, and benefit-sharing expectations.
limitations: Platform-level framework; national legal and ethical requirements still need local review.

sourceLocator: knowledge-base/source-cards/microbial-genomics-implementation-collection.md
passageSummary: The Microbial Genomics implementation collection includes a bioinformatics implementation paper emphasizing standardized, reproducible, validated, auditable, scalable, portable, and secure analysis in public-health laboratory contexts.
limitations: Needs conversion into concrete implementation checklists before being used as operational instructions.
